reviews.review_text: So good, I can't stop nubbing it. Wonderous game and very replayable.

The physics change a while ago made this game go from a fun numbers-go-up experience to a borderline rage game where you wonder how Nubby could have POSSIBLY gone the path that he did. If you come into this game expecting Peggle-like physics, don't. It's a shame because most of my hours were before that patch and it was genuinely some of the biggest fun I've had. Now the game just feels majorly unsatisfying and more like a chore to play.
An engaging engine-building game with a unique and fun style.
This game is so addicting when trying to max my score that my son requests to watch it, great game to just kick back and break the numbers.

the digital equivalent of a fidget toy

have this on 1/4 of the screen, mindlessly playing it to have something visual while listening to a podcast
great for 5e
The game is great but for the past few months i haven't been able to even open up the game because it crashes and shows an error screen, and this bug hasn't been fixed for almost a year

Nubby's Number Factory is the winner for my favorite game to exist. Seeing this purple blob buddy of mine fall and hit several pegs while watching the numbers go up will always fill me with joy. It's plinko-style roguelike gameplay never fails to make me smile. This game has such a nostalgic feel to me and the gameplay is fun and makes you think a little. It is complex yet easy to understand and learn and even then there is an official wiki you can look at. Not to mention the art style and sound deign is silly and whimsical. All of the items, food, supervisors and peg types make the game feel different every time you play and with the challenges and achievements they offer increased difficulty and their own cool mechanics. Being able to buy or unlock skins for your nubby, items, and foods also add another layer of customization that just makes the game more goofy and charming and gives something to strive for. They even have steam point shop items to customize your steam profile and send special emojis as well as having trading cards you can get from playing the game with some nubby related art on them. They also have a discord server for the community to interact and socialize with people in there being very nice. This game is very well priced and WAY worth the 4.99 it takes to buy it and more. It even has a mobile version for ios and android for non pc gamers. I am happy and proud to say that I am a fan of this game and can not wait for what is to come. The team has done some amazing work here and this game needs to be seen more than it is. In conclusion I highly recommend Nubby's Number Factory and believe that YOU whoever is reading this should give it a try if you haven't already. So go out there pop some pegs and make Nubby proud.

Insanely addicting gameplay. Spectacular take on peggle. Makes hours feel like minutes while you whack balls and collect silly items until its just unfair (my most recent run forced me to end the round manually every round since the game would just basically have a stroke until I got a x99999 restock). Would recommend to anyone who wants a new addiction*

*dont hold me responsible for addiction xx
Fun silly game where it's all about building huge combos with the item and skill synergies. Cool art style. Numbers happen.

As a proud employee of the factory, it's an honor and a privilege to work at the number factory. I love it so much I don't even accept a salary. My only complaint is that the warehouse manager keeps stealing the cheese I bring for lunch.

Nubby's Number Factory is pure heroin.

I physically couldn't stop playing until I reached level 1500 and completely broke it with an error pop-up. This is the currently the highest level you possibly get to before the game breaks.

The visuals, sound effects, gameplay loop, and overall atmosphere are incredibly unique.

It's extremely clear that the developer was incredibly passionate about what they were cooking up here.

The only downside to the experience is I wish that the levels were truly endless. After you hit level 1500 you can always restart and experiment with different builds, though.

This is truly the best numbers go up title on the market.

This is without a doubt the best game i have ever played. While very overstimulating, it is incredibly rewarding, and feels incredible when you are doing good. I wholeheartedly reccomend this game to everyone. For how little it costs, I would consider this game an absolute steal. This game has changed my life. Thank you, MogDogBlog Productions.

Bought it right before the absolute hypetrain that hit it a year ago, played a bit but didn't really get into it too much because I didn't give it too much of a chance. Come back a year later to it and decided to actually learn the mechanics a bit and see how it goes. My first hour after coming back was pretty boring I won't lie I was barely getting past the first zone but then I discovered that item synergies are a really huge and important thing in this game and since then I haven't been able to put it down.

It is the perfect mix of style and substance, the style of this game is something you rarely see. The closest games I can think of that have a sort of similar style are games like hypnospace outlaw, Moon, and maybe the 3d models of Picayune dreams? There is just isn't stuff like this on the market, it absolutely stands out and for good reason. You can feel the absolute love put into this by the creator. In terms of substance, I won't say it has an insane item variety but I would say its good enough for you to enjoy the game. Most items can be upgraded to have different effects so that adds more variability and you have like 4 or 5 different shops in the game as well to get different types of items. Think of it as like the item variety in hades I (I think overall hades might have more item variety with the end game upgrades and such). Once you get a good item synergy going I swear its the same exact feeling of having a really good run in Isaac or risk of rain going, I was searching for something that has a similar feeling for so long and I would have to say this is really close to that feeling of thrill and I am glad to say I have found it here. 

I have seen all the balatro comaparisons but I personally haven't played a lot of balatro so I can't really make comparisons between the two

Overall, its worth it considering how cheap it is

this game is actually peak
lots of replayability and good items
some item builds can absolutely break the game, this was worth it
11/10

Nubby's is a pretty good plinko roguelike. The graphics are that incredibly zany early 2k internet wacky fomo or whatnot. It has a really great aesthetic that matches the Wild Tangent studio bargain bin games of the past.

Fundamentally, for a roguelike, it doesn't drop the ball despite the namesake, but only the aesthetic pushes itself to stand out over the gameplay mechanics.
The items lack a meta cognition. In most of the genre, you have a variety of strategies to take on the situation, but here it is extremely limited. You'll find yourself fishing for the same strategy because they blend together in mechanical theory. 
Halving a peg is actually not halving a peg, its popping a peg. However, that popping doesnt matter outside of point accrual because it needs a different item to double it. Very quickly the mechanical difference between each blend together. The black market items, a pool of items meant to guide your synergy, often aren't worth picking over the two or three in the pool that are mechanically unique; like having more nubbies or lowering your total score cap. I'm not really incentivized to jump through hoops, to the point that it makes the difficulty in the challenges nonexistent, outside of not actively reading what is going to happen before hand.

The different 'Tony' characters shakes it up a bit, specifically Baloney Tony. You see, because of the vague-characterization of my point above, the game sets itself up to feel like you're breaking it, or meant to snowball easily. Lunch-Meat man does a really good job at expanding on this by randomizing the traits that cause your items to trigger. This keeps the randomization aspect that roguelikes like to throw in, but capitalizes on the express synergies. There are some other Tony that actively reward you jumping through strange hoops, but its quite fun none the less.

One aspect it does really well is the visual noise. When you get the number machine churning, the game starts blasting visual effects at you and it's a lot of fun. In the later ranks of endless the overlapping looks really cool.

Overall, while Nubby doesn't attempt to push the ball far, its an incredible little roguelike with a very zany aesthetic.  It provides a decent amount of moments where synergies line up and it feels like things click, but tends to blur together outside of a few different Tony.
I'd suggest waiting for this to receive a content update to address the gameplay stagnation. Otherwise, it's worth its incredibly low pricetag.

Poop butt carried the early rounds, Two Headed Turtle, Broken Socket, Jake the Snake, all the fallen warriors that died in battle, Nubbing our Numbers. Their lives won’t go in vain, I’ll avenge them, I’ll Nub my Numbers in that Factory till the day I die, the sun will not explode on my watch and I am Nubbing it so hard.

This game is so good that it became my daily challenge brain buster for about a month each day. I've now bought this game about 4-5 times. Both on steam and mobile. GUYS YOU CAN NUB IT ON THE GO!? This game is so addicting that I've dragged all of my friends into the nub zone with me. Its been my favorite game for the past year or so. I've showed this game to family and friends and the wide message I get from each is that this game is "Stimulation the game." I've now played this game for 100 hours as of posting this review AND I'M STILL COMING BACK FOR MORE. Thank you MogDog, you are my hero.

It's a great little weird roguelite like if peggle and balatro had a baby that was raised by a random adultswim short that would be playing on the tv when you would wake up on the couch at 2am as a kid. 

I will say, if you do get a good combination of items early on, the run is basically over as you can always get an infinite score. So there are times where in order to complete the run you just are shooting nubby over and over again for like twenty minutes straight with no actual gameplay because no matter what you do, you win. The completionist in me is able to look past that fact, but once I get finished with the achievements, it's likely I probably will not come back to this game.

Pure addiction. Very creative game and developers. Much thought, detail was put into each facet, including the balance of each item and perk. The skins are amazing, and the lore is incredible. Truly the game I've been missing from my library.

I really enjoyed playing nubbys number factory during the tony parts of the game. Every tony had their own challenge and reward for playing that specific one and each item in the game having its own niche in some way while having synergies with other items makes every run feel special and its very fun to win with an item you saw alot of potential in. The art style and the OST of the game are amazing as well along with the general idea of the game being pretty genius to be honest

However, i HESITATED giving the game a positive review due to one specific thing in the game which are the challenges. Some of the challenges were very fun type of challenges (one shot, short on change, nubbe-locke, etc) but there was others that were honestly ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ horrible. For an example, nubby trials 5 and expiration date are the most RNG dependent THINGS I've ever played. With expiration date whenever you have a low timer on the items you have, you are on the mercy of the item shop rng and the loss you get from getting bad luck doesn't feel like your fault at all as you couldn't really do anything if half of your items are gone because of it. 

With nubby trials 5 it is a bit of a different story. I haven't beat it yet (i plan to some day hopefully) but from the entire afternoon i spent trying to beat it, it feels like such an rng mess. At least with expiration date you could pick certain perks that give you an upper hand regardless of the item roll, but with trial 5 all you do is restart early game over and over and hope you get two headed turtle or else that run isn't one worth playing. Usually nubbys ball rng isnt that bad in most games as it didn't matter usually, but trial 5 is so centered around getting the highest number peg and with nubbys insanely varied bouncing and peg jumbling rng, the run can basically just end to no fault of your own 

In conclusion i love the game when it does more "one good, one bad" type of game modes like with the tonys, but i felt like alot of the super hard challenges weren't hard because they were skill based but more because you're at the mercy of like 4-5 rng factors that when you realize them make it significantly less fun for me in my opinion. I do want to 100% this game (i didn't spend 80 hours on it for nothing lol) out of love for the game but man it reeeealy sucks in certain aspects.
So fun and addicting! I get so entranced that time seems to just slip away... :)

It's a fun game but it gets boring kind of quickly. I am leaving a negative review only because there is no mixed option. Nubby currently has 97% overwhelmingly positive reviews which I find very weird.

For me Nubby's Number Factory is a 7,5/10 game, mainly because of the vibes. The roguelike mechanics aren't that good. 

Its more about numbers and combos between items than the pachinko mechanics. 

There are no items/perks that for example make Nubby bounce more or reduce the gravity and stuff like that, physics are sometimes straight up weird and annoying. 

Player can almost always brute force the same build, and is never punished for that because every build has the same core, doubling/halving the numbers (there are challenges that limit that but only a few). 

There is hardly any item variety so its almost always just the early game that differs from run to run. Also I've only seen 2 different bosses, Tony and the blood rain, both of them are easily countered by happy seal + tardigide + something to pop the numbers combo. 

The games only difficulty is in the early game and its mostly rng. early game centered items don't really combo with eachother, they are mostly perk depended. Late game is always a steamroll with 9999x round goal score. 

There are different supervisors that are supposed to change up the game (like characters in Issac or Decks in Balatro) but most of them are literally played the same once you pass round 40 (final boss is on round 80 and after then the player can decide to go endless).

There are also special pegs that have special effects but they also don't matter at all since many good late game builds (mainly just 1 build which is always good) just straight up clear entire board anyway no matter what the situation, also there is no way to avoid hitting them since Nubby bounces unpredictably and there are no items to affect Nubbys movement. 

Game is carried by vibes, but its a chill experience once you get through the early rounds. It's a fun game for a couple of runs or if someone is bored and wants a cheap game to just watch the numbers go brrr. I don't recommend buying it for someone interested in a challenge or for someone who is looking to create fun strategic builds.

Nubby's Number Factory is a really nice indie game.

There’s no real story, you simply play as a ball and make other balls explode to earn points. The gameplay is very simple, but that’s exactly what makes it so fun and addictive.

The visuals are also very simple, matching the gameplay style, 
but that doesn’t make the game bad or boring. In fact, it’s the complete opposite, 
the clean design makes the gameplay even more enjoyable and easy to focus on.

The main downside is that the game can become very time-consuming. Getting all the achievements can take days, and some of them are really annoying to unlock. The layout of the numbered balls can also feel frustrating at times, but that’s simply part of the game’s design.

The gameplay is a solid 8.6/10. There isn’t much more to say, it’s a very good game if you’re bored and just want to play something simple and relaxing.

If you love Peggle or Zuma, you’ll probably love this too.

Nubby's Number Factory has given me so many opportunities in my Career. I would like to thank them for the boost on my resume.

Nubtastic!

Story is N/A 

Setting is N/A

Visually, it matches the bizarre nature of something like Hypnospace Outlaw. Performance was solid throughout and I didn't run into any bugs or glitches. I also played this on steam deck and it works pretty well, but I kinda comes down to preference if you like the feel of mouse more than track pad. The Art Direction is something ripped straight from the 2000's which helps it stand out from other contemporaries in the genre.

Sound and Audio are the right vibe with the soundtrack being really chill and relaxing to listen to with the Audio design getting real wacky with some of the items that are on show.

In terms of Gameplay, its a great time to do a quick run or two! The game is essentially a Pachinko Roguelike which works really well for this size of an experience with how easy it can be to pick up and drop the title. There is a really well diverse array of items and perks that help spice things up, but mainly its just a really fun time. While Peglin exists, I think this is the true Peggle successor and was all round (pun intended) fun.

Overall, Nubby's Number Factory is a very welcome addition to the roguelike scene and would easily recommend it to anyone who's looking for that game where they can go "just one more run"!

Beyond the absurdity of exploding pegs, meat-orbs, butts of poop, and that ever pesky Tony; there lies a considerably profound message about resilience, perseverance, and the human condition.
Over and over again Nubby, our hero in this story, is launched into utter chaos.  He is repeatedly slammed into walls or blasted with nuclear waste from a nearby fuel rod.  Sometimes, Nubby is dead within a second.  Others, Nubby is thrown into a completely incomprehensible disaster of ichor streams and snot-shots that to continue would seem like an impossibility.
And yet, every single time.  Nubby gets back up.
Nubby doesn't whine, or complain.  Nubby doesn't hesitate.  The launch button is pressed once again and Nubby is forcefully ejected into the fresh hell that the number factory has in store for one simple reason.
The numbers must grow.  Quotas must be met.  And the factory must continue.
After a while of playing, this simple loop began to become inspirational.
The more I played, the more I realized that Nubby can represent something that is profoundly familiar in everyday life.  The majority of people are not heroic, at least not in the traditional sense.  The average person won't be a legendary warrior or "The Chosen One".  We are just ordinary people, everyday trying our best to meet arbitrary, impossible quotas in systems that are larger than ourselves.  Everyday, we wake up and throw ourselves back into an uncertain, or even unfair world; school, work, relationships, responsibilities, failures, exhaustion, hoping we can somehow keep ourselves from collapsing.
And like Nubby, we get bounced around in unexpected directions from time to time.
Sometimes, life may feel like being force fed a lentil and watching as everything you built around you is changed without warning.  That plan for a future career, a house you wanted to buy, a family you planned to start, thrown into chaos seemingly in an instant.  Sometimes you build an incredible setup, only for one bad event to destroy the run.  Sometimes you feel like you are one missed quota away from the sun exploding.
But Nubby quietly teaches us something important through the mask of absurdity: progress is not about never failing, but about relaunching despite failure.
Nubby is constantly, violently hurdled to a gruesome death.  However, death itself can become a path to success.  Entire runs can be built around this philosophy.  The lunar perk rewards persistence over time, Ouroboros quite literally rewards Nubby's accumulated suffering.  Even disaster can become the fuel to bring you to success.
This idea can transcend the game entirely.
People fail classes.  They lose jobs.  A relationship collapses.  Plans fall apart.  Entire periods of life can feel like every step is a catastrophic misplay.  But Nubby teaches us that true value is not determined by avoiding hardship, but by being able to continue despite it.
There is also something oddly beautiful about how insignificant Nubby can appear compared to that which surrounds him.  He is small, replaceable even.  Yet the stability of the sun relies on Nubby's ability to fulfill a quota and the perseverance to keep going.  This mirrors reality in a way few other games do.  Society is held together not by grand heroes, but by ordinary people who keep going and working through difficult jobs and environments despite exhaustion or uncertainty.
Parents.
Teachers.
Tradesmen.
People quietly meeting their quotas every day so the world can continue to function.
And through this game you can experience the failure, the rebuilding, and the inevitable satisfaction of a broken run that was grown from the ashes of a failure.
Soon, Nubby becomes more than a funny little ball.  Nubby becomes a symbol.
Every time Nubby is sent back into the pegs is a reminder that failure is only temporary.  Every seemingly impossible quota that is overcome in inexplicable ways is evidence that success can emerge from chaos.
That is why Nubby's Number Factory is more than just a silly little ball game.  Beneath the wacky items and dopamine-filled number popping, is a sincere meditation on perseverance.  Nubby teaches that even when the board layout is sub-optimal, the item synergy is atrocious, and the run feels over, there is still meaning in launching one more time.
